Now I have a good picture. Let me put together some compelling demo ideas for you:



Démo Applications for IFT6390 (Linear/Ridge/Logistic Regression)


Here are some engaging applications that move from theory to practice, with varying levels of complexity:



1. Linear Regression: Building Energy Consumption


Perfect tie-in to your Trane/BrainBox research


Dataset: Building energy vs. heating/cooling degree days


Plot monthly kWh vs. HDD (heating degree days)

Fit linear regression: Energy = β₀ + β₁·HDD

Key insight: Slope = building's thermal sensitivity, intercept = baseload (lighting, equipment)

Live demo: Change the λ in ridge → show how coefficient shrinks


Why it's compelling: Students can relate to their own apartment's heating bill. The model has a physical interpretation (W/°C).



2. Ridge Regression: Baseball/NBA Salary Prediction


The "Hitters" dataset from ISLR is canonical


Dataset: Hitters (322 MLB players, 20 features → salary)


Show OLS overfitting with 20 correlated features

Ridge shrinks coefficients toward zero

Plot: Coefficient paths vs. λ (the classic ridge trace)

Cross-validation to pick optimal λ


Why it's compelling: Students immediately understand multicollinearity (career stats are correlated with recent stats). The Moneyball angle makes it fun.



3. Logistic Regression: Breast Cancer Diagnosis


Wisconsin Diagnostic dataset — built into sklearn


from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
data = load_breast_cancer()  # 569 samples, 30 features


Binary classification: malignant (0) vs. benign (1)

Features: radius_mean, texture_mean, perimeter, area, smoothness...

Key demo: 
Interpret coefficients as log-odds

Show decision boundary in 2D (e.g., radius vs. texture)

ROC curve / AUC


Why it's compelling: Life-or-death stakes, interpretable coefficients matter clinically ("a 1-unit increase in X multiplies the odds by exp(β)").



4. Logistic Regression: Spam Detection


Classic NLP application


Dataset: SMS Spam Collection (Kaggle) — 5,574 messages


Vectorize text → TF-IDF features

Fit logistic regression

Key demo: Look at top positive/negative coefficients
"free", "winner", "urgent" → strong spam indicators

"mom", "tonight" → ham indicators


Why it's compelling: Shows how simple models work surprisingly well. Students can test with their own text.



5. California Housing Price Prediction


Built into sklearn, great for comparing all three


from sklearn.datasets import fetch_california_housing


20,640 districts, 8 features (median income, house age, location...)

Progressive demo:
Simple linear regression: price ~ median_income (R² ≈ 0.47)

Multiple regression with all features (R² ≈ 0.60)

Ridge regression to handle multicollinearity

Bonus: Plot predictions on a map (lat/long) — very visual!


Why it's compelling: Everyone understands house prices. Geographic visualization is striking.



6. Climate Application: Degree Day Regression


Topical and policy-relevant


Model how climate change affects building energy:


Fit cooling_energy ~ CDD and heating_energy ~ HDD

Use future climate projections (IPCC degree day data)

Predict % change in energy consumption by 2050


Why it's compelling: Directly relevant to climate policy. Students see how simple regression informs major decisions.



My Recommendation for a Live Demo


Start with California Housing because:


Data loads in one line

You can show the full progression: simple → multiple → ridge

The correlation matrix shows why ridge helps

Map visualization is memorable


Then switch to Breast Cancer for logistic regression — the medical stakes make coefficient interpretation viscerally important.

Now let me compile a concrete list with actual download links:


Web Fetch: Fetched: Student Performance


Now I have great concrete info. Let me create a practical summary with real datasets:



Concrete Datasets for Your Demos


Here are real datasets with download links that work perfectly for linear/ridge/logistic regression demos:



1. Student Performance (Portuguese Schools)


The story: Does alcohol consumption affect grades? Does parental education matter? Study time vs. going out with friends?

Download
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/320/student+performance
Size
395 students (math) + 649 (Portuguese)
Target
Final grade G3 (0-20 scale)
Features
30 variables: alcohol (Dalc, Walc), studytime, absences, romantic relationship, internet access, family size...

Why it's great for class:


Students relate instantly (it's about them)

Alcohol consumption vs. grades: immediate engagement

studytime has weak correlation — counterintuitive!

Good for logistic too: predict pass/fail (G3 ≥ 10)


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo
student = fetch_ucirepo(id=320)
X, y = student.data.features, student.data.targets



2. Medical Insurance Costs


The story: Why does one person pay $1,000/year and another $40,000? Smoking, BMI, age, region.

Download
https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/mirichoi0218/insurance
Direct CSV
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stedy/Machine-Learning-with-R-datasets/master/insurance.csv
Size
1,338 people
Target
charges (annual medical costs in $)
Features
age, sex, bmi, children, smoker, region

Why it's great:


Smoking has a massive effect — perfect for showing coefficient interpretation

Non-linear interactions: smoker × BMI interaction is real

Ridge helps when you add polynomial features

Policy-relevant: "Is it fair to charge smokers more?"


import pandas as pd
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stedy/Machine-Learning-with-R-datasets/master/insurance.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)



3. Bike Sharing (Washington DC)


The story: How does weather affect bike rentals? Can we predict demand for city planning?

Download
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/275/bike+sharing+dataset
Size
17,379 hourly records (2 years)
Target
cnt (bike rentals per hour)
Features
temp, humidity, windspeed, season, hour, holiday, weathersit

Why it's great:


Clear time-of-day patterns (commute peaks at 8am, 6pm)

Temperature has strong positive effect → students see the regression line

Real policy application: Montréal could use this for Bixi!

Weather categories work well for ridge (many dummy variables)


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo
bike = fetch_ucirepo(id=275)



4. Wine Quality (Portuguese Vinho Verde)


The story: Can chemistry predict if a wine is good? What makes wine "excellent"?

Download
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/186/wine+quality
Direct CSV
http://mlr.cs.umass.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/wine-quality/winequality-red.csv
Size
1,599 red wines + 4,898 white wines
Target
quality (score 0-10, mostly 5-7)
Features
11 chemical properties: alcohol, acidity, sulphates, pH, residual sugar...

Why it's great:


Alcohol is the strongest predictor (students find this funny)

Good for logistic: classify quality ≥ 7 as "excellent"

Imbalanced classes: few excellent wines → discuss this

Multicollinearity between acids → ridge helps


import pandas as pd
url = "http://mlr.cs.umass.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/wine-quality/winequality-red.csv"
wine = pd.read_csv(url, sep=';')



5. Breast Cancer Wisconsin (sklearn built-in)


The story: Classify tumors as malignant/benign from cell measurements. Life or death.

Load
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
Size
569 samples
Target
malignant (0) / benign (1)
Features
30 measurements: radius, texture, perimeter, area, smoothness...

Why it's great for logistic regression:


High stakes → coefficient interpretation matters

Achieves ~97% accuracy with logistic regression alone

Perfect for ROC curves, precision/recall discussion

Medical ethics: "What's the cost of a false negative?"


from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

Bike Sharing

Logistic regression

Breast Cancer

Now I have great material. Let me create a focused summary with datasets in your actual domains:



Fresh Datasets in Your Research Domains


Here are datasets you've likely never seen in an intro ML class, directly relevant to your research:



1. Concrete Compressive Strength (Materials)


The story: Predict concrete strength from its recipe — cement, slag, fly ash, water, superplasticizer, aggregates, and curing time (age).

Download
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo; concrete = fetch_ucirepo(id=165)
Direct
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/165/concrete+compressive+strength
Size
1,030 samples
Target
Compressive strength (MPa)
Features
8 inputs: cement, blast furnace slag, fly ash, water, superplasticizer, coarse/fine aggregate (all kg/m³), age (days)

Why it's perfect for your class:


Water-to-cement ratio has physical meaning → students can derive it as a feature

Slag and fly ash are cement substitutes with sustainability implications

Age shows nonlinear strength gain (log transformation helps) — classic curve

Linear regression R² ≈ 0.6, ridge helps with collinear aggregates

Connects to your Trane work on materials and process control


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo

2. Superconductor Critical Temperature (Materials Discovery)


The story: Predict the critical temperature Tc of superconductors from their chemical composition — 21,000+ materials from Japan's SuperCon database.

Download
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo; sc = fetch_ucirepo(id=464)
Direct
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/464/superconductivty+data
Size
21,263 superconductors
Target
Critical temperature Tc (K)
Features
81 features derived from elemental properties: mean atomic mass, mean electron affinity, mean thermal conductivity, weighted entropy of valence, etc.

Why it's perfect:


Real materials discovery — directly connects to your RefGen project

Features are computed from periodic table → interpretable coefficients

Highly skewed target: most Tc < 20K, but some cuprates reach 130K → log transform

Ridge regression is essential (81 correlated features!)

You can discuss: "What makes a high-Tc superconductor?"


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo
sc = fetch_ucirepo(id=464)
X, y = sc.data.features, sc.data.targets
# Target is highly skewed → try log(Tc)



3. Building Energy Efficiency (HVAC/Buildings)


The story: Predict heating and cooling loads from building geometry — glazing area, wall area, roof area, orientation, compactness.

Download
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo; energy = fetch_ucirepo(id=242)
Size
768 building configurations
Targets
Heating Load (Y1) and Cooling Load (Y2) in kWh/m²
Features
8 geometric parameters: relative compactness, surface area, wall area, roof area, overall height, orientation, glazing area, glazing distribution

Why it's perfect:


Simulated from EnergyPlus → clean data, physical meaning

Two targets — you can show multi-output regression

Coefficient interpretation: "Each m² of glazing adds X kWh/m² to cooling load"

Direct connection to your BrainBox/Trane work


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo

4. Hydro-Québec Open Data (Hydropower)


The story: Real-time flows, water levels, and meteorological data from HQ's northern Québec stations.

Portal
https://donnees.hydroquebec.com/explore/dataset/donnees-hydrometriques/
Data
River flows (m³/s), reservoir inflows, water levels, precipitation, snow depth
Coverage
Northern Québec, 15-minute intervals, CSV export available

Demo idea: Download a few months of data, predict tomorrow's inflow from today's precipitation and snow water equivalent — simple regression, very local story.



5. Oak Ridge National Lab HVAC Dataset (HVAC/Controls)


The story: Real measured data from a DOE test building under controlled HVAC experiments — heating tests, cooling tests, free-floating.

Paper
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-022-01858-6
Data
https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.19761416
Size
1-minute intervals, multiple seasons
Features
Weather (outdoor temp, solar radiation), indoor temps by zone, supply/return air temps, airflow rates, energy consumption

Why it's perfect:


Real building data, not simulation

Multiple HVAC operating modes → students can see how control strategy affects energy

You can predict indoor temperature from setpoint + outdoor conditions



6. GloHydroRes — Global Hydropower Dataset (Hydro)


The story: Comprehensive database of 7,775 hydropower plants with reservoir characteristics — head, capacity, dam height, reservoir volume.

Download
https://zenodo.org/records/14526360
Format
CSV and Excel
Features
Plant type (run-of-river vs storage), installed capacity (MW), head (m), dam height, reservoir volume, area, depth

Demo idea: Predict installed capacity from head × flow characteristics → P = ηρgQH regression

Now I have great material for logistic regression. Let me compile the best options for your domains:



Logistic Regression Datasets in Your Domains


Here are fresh classification datasets that tie directly to energy, manufacturing, and materials:



1. Steel Plates Faults (Manufacturing Quality)


The story: Classify surface defects on stainless steel plates into 7 fault types based on 27 geometric measurements of the defect shape.

Download
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo; steel = fetch_ucirepo(id=198)
Size
1,941 fault instances
Target
7 fault types: Pastry, Z_Scratch, K_Scratch, Stains, Dirtiness, Bumps, Other_Faults
Features
27 geometric descriptors: X/Y_Min/Max, Pixels_Areas, Perimeter, Luminosity stats, Steel type (A300/A400), Plate thickness, various shape indices

Why it's perfect:


Binary simplification: Merge into "Scratch-type" vs "Surface-type" defects for intro logistic regression

Features have physical meaning: "Is the defect elongated (high X_Perimeter/Y_Perimeter)? → Scratch"

Steel type (A300 vs A400) as categorical predictor

Multiclass extension: one-vs-rest logistic regression for 7 classes

No images needed — purely tabular, perfect for logistic regression demo


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo
steel = fetch_ucirepo(id=198)
X = steel.data.features
y = steel.data.targets

# For binary: "Is it a scratch-type defect?"
import pandas as pd
y_binary = y.apply(lambda row: 1 if row['Z_Scratch']==1 or row['K_Scatch']==1 else 0, axis=1)



2. Superconductor: High-Tc vs Low-Tc Classification (Materials Discovery)


The story: Classify superconductors as "high-Tc" (≥10K) or "low-Tc" (<10K) from elemental composition features.

Download
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo; sc = fetch_ucirepo(id=464)
Size
21,263 superconductors
Binary target
High-Tc (Tc ≥ 10K) vs Low-Tc (Tc < 10K)
Features
81 elemental property features

Why it's perfect:


Published papers use exactly this 10K threshold for classification

Achieves ~92% accuracy with logistic regression alone!

Coefficient interpretation: "Materials with high mean_atomic_mass are more likely to be high-Tc"

Connects directly to your RefGen work on refrigerant discovery

Class imbalance discussion (most materials are low-Tc)


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo
sc = fetch_ucirepo(id=464)
X = sc.data.features
y = (sc.data.targets['critical_temp'] >= 10).astype(int)  # Binary: high-Tc vs low-Tc



3. Concrete Strength: Pass/Fail Classification (Materials/Construction)


The story: Does this concrete mix meet minimum strength requirements (≥25 MPa for structural use)?

Download
from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo; concrete = fetch_ucirepo(id=165)
Size
1,030 mixes
Binary target
Strength ≥ 25 MPa (structural grade)
Features
Cement, slag, fly ash, water, superplasticizer, aggregates, age

Why it's perfect:


Students already know the regression version — now show classification

Physical threshold: 25 MPa is actual structural concrete requirement

Feature interpretation: "Each kg of cement increases log-odds of passing by X"

Water-cement ratio as derived feature improves model

Discuss: ROC curve, precision-recall for quality control ("cost of false positive vs false negative")


from ucimlrepo import fetch_ucirepo
concrete = fetch_ucirepo(id=165)
X = concrete.data.features
y = (concrete.data.targets['Strength'] >= 25).astype(int)  # Structural grade threshold



4. NASA Turbofan: Will Engine Fail Within N Cycles? (Predictive Maintenance)


The story: Given sensor readings, predict if a jet engine will fail within the next 30 cycles.

Download
https://www.nasa.gov/intelligent-systems-division/discovery-and-systems-health/pcoe/pcoe-data-set-repository/ (C-MAPSS)
Size
100 engines, ~20,000 cycles
Binary target
RUL ≤ 30 cycles (imminent failure)
Features
21 sensor readings + 3 operational settings

Why it's perfect:


Classic predictive maintenance problem

Transform RUL regression into classification: "Needs maintenance NOW?"

Sensor features: temperatures, pressures, speeds — all physical

Imbalanced classes (most cycles are not near failure)

Directly relevant to your aircraft maintenance scheduling work


import pandas as pd
train = pd.read_csv('train_FD001.txt', sep='\s+', header=None)

5. LBNL HVAC Fault Detection Dataset (HVAC/Controls)


The story: Classify HVAC operating states as "normal" or "faulty" from sensor readings — chiller plants, AHUs, RTUs.

Download
https://data.openei.org/submissions/5763
Systems
Chiller plant, AHU, RTU, Boiler, Fan coil
Target
Normal vs Fault (or specific fault types)
Features
Temperatures, pressures, flow rates, valve positions, power consumption

Why it's perfect:


Directly relevant to your Trane/BrainBox work

Real building data (not simulation)

Multiple fault types: sensor bias, stuck valve, coil fouling, refrigerant undercharge

Binary first: "Is there ANY fault?" → then multiclass: "Which fault?"

Time series aspect: can aggregate features (mean, std over window)
